idea.hook: Imagine a planet where a 'day' lasts decades! 🤯
facts: Uranus spins almost completely on its side, with an extreme axial tilt of 98 degrees.
This means during half of its 84-Earth-year orbit, one pole faces the Sun, experiencing continuous 'daylight' for 42 Earth years.
The opposite pole endures 42 Earth years of total darkness, an unbroken 'night'.
idea.payoff: A single 'day' or 'night' at Uranus's poles can last longer than an entire human lifetime!
idea.title: Uranus: Day or Night for 42 Years?! 🤯🪐

scenes.text: Imagine a planet where a 'day' lasts decades! 🤯
scenes.visual: A dark space background with subtle distant stars. Uranus slowly rotates, slightly tilted, in the center of the frame.

scenes.text: Uranus spins almost completely on its side, with an extreme axial tilt of 98 degrees.
scenes.visual: Uranus prominently displayed, spinning on its side. An animated line highlights its 98-degree axial tilt. The sun is faintly visible in the upper corner.

scenes.text: One pole faces the Sun for 42 Earth years of continuous 'daylight'.
scenes.visual: Uranus, still tilted. The top pole is brightly illuminated, with an arrow pointing to it, while the rest of the planet shows gradual shadowing. A subtle orbital path animation.

scenes.text: The opposite pole endures 42 Earth years of total darkness, an unbroken 'night'.
scenes.visual: Uranus, with the bottom pole now completely dark and cold-looking, receiving no light. An arrow points to the dark pole. The sun is on the opposite side of the planet's orbit.

scenes.text: A single 'day' or 'night' on Uranus can last longer than an entire human lifetime!
scenes.visual: A dramatic shot of Uranus, split perfectly down the middle, half brilliantly lit by the sun, half in profound, inky darkness. Emphasizes the extreme contrast. Stars twinkle in the background.

youtube.title: Uranus: Day or Night for 42 Years?! 🤯🪐
